这是我的时事通讯的一期，重点关注互联网的心理和技术方面，尤其是远程工作、在线经济和认知负荷。在下方注册以加入俱乐部 随着 7 月的严寒让热浪消退，我们搬到了乡下几个星期。在那里，我们可以很好地看到邻居的农场。当那些小农仍然存在时，我们欣喜若狂地观察农村生活方式的节奏。当奶牛喝满牛奶时，我的邻居不得不给她挤奶。当黑麦在七月成熟时，他每天工作 16 个小时，以镰刀、扫地和耙子。他早上 5 点收集鸡蛋，晚上 8 点太阳不再那么炎热时给蔬菜浇水。无数文章建议保持“工作与生活的平衡”。下午 5 点下班，关闭工作电话/电子邮件，享受你的“生活”。设定适当的界限至关重要——文章一致指出。保持心理卫生。我的邻居太忙了，没空坐在办公室里翻阅互联网上的文章，所以他没有听说过工作与生活的平衡。他做他需要做的事，他介于两者之间。他看到自己的劳动成果，花几个小时看着黑麦长大。我有时很羡慕他。在 1800 年代，90% 的美国人口生活在农场上，他们的 WFH 设置摇摆不定。他们是如何在没有精神崩溃和哈佛商业评论文章称赞严格的工作与生活平衡的情况下生存的？我相信我们对工作与生活平衡的争论是错误的。我们应该专注于保持动态平衡，而不是在生活和工作之间引入更严格的墙，就像我附近的农民一样。
做需要做的事情，不要因为时钟告诉你而坐在原地。来自 Ness Labs 的 Anne-Laure Le Cunff 在她的文章“工作与生活平衡的问题”中谈到了这个问题，从这句话本身开始：“这是一个令人衰弱的短语，因为它意味着存在严格的权衡。现实是，如果我在家开心，我就会带着巨大的能量来到办公室。如果我在工作中感到快乐，我就会带着巨大的能量回家。它实际上是一个圆；这不是平衡。”我对措辞没有这样的问题，但我认为我们错了，我们认为“平衡”意味着生活各部分之间的高墙。但平衡可能意味着一种动态平衡（如“全神贯注的力量”）——当你的工作+生活馅饼的某一面变得过大时，你会做出补偿——来自 Anne-Laure 的文章：有一天，你的一个孩子可能会生病；改天，你可能需要当场更换同事；又一天，你可能会感到生产力的爆发，完成了很多工作，你可以好好休息一下。夏天工作和冬天工作的感觉不一样；睡眠不足时会感觉更难工作；当您的同事提供帮助时，您会感觉更轻松。这些都是你无法控制的不断变化的因素，时间有点残酷。当您玩得开心时它会飞过去，当您计算轮班结束的分钟数时，它会一直拖延下去。所以你可以决定多享受一点乐趣，工作就会不那么累。任何工作场所都可以提供：如果您试图在工作与生活之间建立严格的界限，那么您就会将工作视为敌人，并且会逃避第一次获得的机会。
像瘟疫一样避免 BS（如在 BuSywork 中）。你不应该为了“证明你的忠诚”而不断加班。但是如果你有机会做一些很棒的事情，不要因为下午 5 点就把它扔掉。在成为团队负责人之前，我聘请了一名 VA 来训练我的代表团肌肉。它教会我放弃微观管理的倾向。它为我的团队和我的家人带来了回报。西蒙·萨里斯 (Simon Sarris) 比以往任何时候都更清楚地阐明了我的谈话要点之一：学校教孩子们成为生活的被动消费者。我们似乎对政治（公众）的想象如此肤浅，以至于无法想象如何对待孩子，尤其是聪明的孩子。我们在整个青春期都未能适当地尊重它们，因此我们将它们设计为在此期间毫无用处。但它不一定是这样。事实上，我们可以教他们两方面的能力，以及课程中的知识：世界的秘密在于它是一个非常具有延展性的地方，我们必须确保人们学习这一点，永远不要忘记顺序：学习自然是结果做的。您不必等待专业人士告诉您如何制作东西，您可以制作东西。开始打字
Typosquatters 注册类似于已知的、现有的域，希望有人拼错地址并最终出现在他们的网站上。 Gail.com 的所有者从她的丈夫那里收到了域名，结果发现很多人最终都在那里而不是 gmail：在 2020 年，该页面共获得 5,950,012 次点击，平均每天 16,257 次。仅查看独特点击次数，我们总共收到了 1,295,284 次，平均每天有 3,539 次独特点击。有时，我们会受到 Twitter 轰炸，每天可能会有数万名访客。例如，在 2020 年 7 月 21 日，我们收到了 109,316 次点击。问：您是否有兴趣通过 gail.com 获利？ A：不，但感谢您的提问。问：你不知道你可以投一些广告来赚钱吗？答：是的，我知道了，谢谢。对于那些觉得生活中需要更多广告的人，请看下面我们时髦的电子前沿基金会广告。如果您相信免费互联网，请考虑单击该链接并向 EFF 捐款。 1997 年 2 月 23 日，巨大的海浪袭击了 Tokio Express 货轮，将 62 个集装箱冲入海中，将近 500 万块乐高积木掉入大海。 24 年后，我们仍在寻找它。在丢失的碎片中，有绿龙，在海滩漫步者中非常珍贵。 Lego Lost at Sea 项目记录了这些发现，但后来扩展到康沃尔海滩上所有的塑料垃圾。我写的是互联网的心理和技术方面，重点是远程工作、在线经济和认知负荷。每逢星期一。

note_en:
This is an issue of my newsletter focusing on the psychological and technical aspects of the Internet, particularly remote work, online economy, and cognitive load.  Sign up below to join the club
 As the gripping cold conceded to the heatwaves in July, we moved to the countryside for a few weeks. There, we have a good view of our neighbors’ farm. While those small farmers are still around, we’re ecstatic to observe the rhythms of the rural lifestyle.
 When the cow moos full of milk, my neighbor has to milk her. When the rye is ripe in July, he works 16 hours a day to scythe, sweep, and rake. He collects his chickens’ eggs at 5 am and waters the vegetables at 8 pm when the sun is not so scorching anymore.
 Countless articles recommend keeping “Work-Life Balance”. Leave your job at 5 PM, turn off the work phone/email and enjoy your “Life”. It is crucial to set proper boundaries – the articles state in unison. Keep your mental hygiene.
 My neighbor is too busy to sit in the office scrolling articles on the Internet, so he hasn’t heard about Work-Life Balance. He does what he needs to, and he rests in between. He sees the fruits of his labor and spends hours watching the rye grow. I envy him sometimes.
  In the 1800s, 90 percent of the US population lived on a farm, rocking their WFH setups. How did they all survive without mental breakdowns and Harvard Business Review articles praising strict Work-Life Balance?
 I believe we have the work-life balance debate wrong. Instead of introducing more rigid walls between Life and Work, we should focus on keeping a dynamic equilibrium – just like my neighborhood farmer.
 Do things that need to be done, and stop sitting in place just because the clock tells you to.
 Anne-Laure Le Cunff from Ness Labs touches on that issue in her article “ The problem with work-life balance“, starting with the phrase itself:
 “That’s a debilitating phrase because it implies there’s a strict trade-off. And the reality is, if I am happy at home, I come into the office with tremendous energy. And if I am happy at work, I come home with tremendous energy. It actually is a circle; it’s not a balance.”
 I don’t have such issues with the phrasing, but I think we have it wrong where we think “balance” means tall walls between parts of life. But balance may mean a dynamic equilibrium (as in “ Power of Full engagement“) – when one side of your work+life pie gets outsized, you compensate – from Anne-Laure’s article:
 One day, one of your kids may get sick; another day, you may need to replace a colleague on the spot; yet another day, you may feel a burst of productivity and get so much done you can take a really nice break. It feels different to work in the summer than in the winter; it feels harder to work when you lack sleep; it feels easier when your colleagues are being helpful. These are ever changing factors you can’t control,
  Time is a bit cruel. It flies by when you’re having fun, and drags on forever when you’re counting minutes for your shift to end. So you can just decide to have a little more fun, and work will be less exhausting. Any workplace can provide:
  If you’re trying to introduce strict boundaries between work & life, you’re going to treat your job as the enemy and something to run away from the first chance you’ll get.
 Avoid BS ( As in BuSywork) like the plague. You shouldn’t do constant overtime just to “prove your loyalty”. But if you get a chance to do something awesome, don’t throw it away just because it’s 5 PM.
   Before becoming a Team Lead, I hired a VA to train my delegation muscle. It has taught me to let go of micromanaging tendencies. It paid off for my Team and my Family.
   Simon Sarris has articulated one of my talking points much better than I ever could: The school teaches children to be passive consumers of life.
 We seem to have a political (public) imagination so shallow that it cannot conceive of what to even do with children, especially smart children. We fail to properly respect them all the way through adolescence, so we have engineered them to be useless in the interim.
 But it does not have to be this way. In fact, we can teach them BOTH agency, and knowledge from the curriculum:
 The secret of the world is that it is a very malleable place, we must be sure that people learn this, and never forget the order: Learning is naturally the consequence of doing.
  You don’t have to wait for professionals to tell you how to make stuff, you can just make stuff. Start typing
  Typosquatters register domains similar to known, existing ones hoping that somebody misspells the address and end up on their site instead.
 The owner of  Gail.com received the domain from her husband, and it turns out many people end up there instead of gmail:
 In 2020 this page received a total of 5,950,012 hits, which is an average of 16,257 per day. Looking at just unique hits, we received a total of 1,295,284, for an average of 3,539 unique hits per day. Occasionally, we get Twitter-bombed and may get several tens of thousands of visitors a day. As an example, on July 21st 2020 we received 109,316 hits.
  Q: Are you interested in monetizing gail.com?  A: No, but thanks for asking.  Q: Don’t you know that you could throw some ads up and make money?  A: Yes, I know, thank you. For those who feel they need more advertising in their life, please have a look at our swanky Electronic Frontier Foundation ad below. If you believe in a free Internet, please consider clicking on the link and donating to the EFF.
    On Feb 23rd 1997, nearly 5 million bits of Lego fell into the ocean when a huge wave hit the cargo ship Tokio Express, washing 62 containers overboard. We’re still finding it 24 years later. Among the pieces lost were green dragons, highly prized among beachcombers.
 Lego Lost at Sea project documents those findings but has since expanded to all plastic debree washing out on Cornish beaches.

title: 农民总是在家工作

note: 这是一篇关于以写作艺术为生的博客文章。比如说，独立游戏。所以期待一定程度的绝望。在过去，许多有才华的人都想成为艺术家。然而，他们中很少有人为了过上体面的生活而出售他们的艺术品。所以他们会得到一个赞助人。赞助人是一个被你逗乐的非常富有的人。他们为你提供他们财富的一小部分，而你继续作为艺术家存在以取悦他们。 （这个系统在许多世纪前就出现了，早在现代资本主义存在之前，所以把你的资本主义 Iz Bad 热拍留到阳光明媚的一天。）许多非常有才华的人正在编写独立游戏，但竞争是令人心碎的，大多数游戏玩家不甚至懒得玩他们买的游戏。我们这些有设计能力但没有成功的人必须找到顾客。目前有许多路线可以惠顾。假设你想写一个关于一个垂死的老人想要学会微笑的独立 2-D roguelike 平台游戏。然而，与此同时，你不想生活在贫困中。这是通往希望的三大途径。 1. 众筹（例如 Kickstarter、Fig） Kickstarter 实现了一个真正的奇迹：他们想出了如何将善意货币化。我是 Kickstarter 的忠实粉丝。我目前正在 Kickstart 重新制作我们的邪教经典 Geneforge。进行得顺利。我写这篇博文是为了让这项工作得到更多关注。 Kickstarter 既是人们预购您的游戏的一种方式，也是一种寻找愿意为单个副本支付更多金钱的顾客的方式。有一次，我的粉丝每场比赛给了我 20-30 美元。感谢 Kickstarter，长期以来一直是我粉丝的更富有的人现在可以支付更多费用并获得更多回报。它们使我们能够继续编写游戏。在过去，如果你是一位剧作家的赞助人，你实际上可以在表演时坐在舞台上，一边玩一边取笑。如果你是我的忠实赞助人，我会让你给我一些想法来投入游戏。您可以在我们的 Kickstarter 上支付更多费用来命名游戏的角色或设计元素。 （顺便说一下，我真的很喜欢这个。我们从我们的支持者那里得到的想法质量非常好，写起来很有趣。但我不会让你这样做，除非你帮我买食物给我孩子们。）我希望我们能从现在开始我们编写的每一个游戏。这确实对我们的业务产生了很大的影响。如果这方面的某些事情让你生气，请记住它基本上只是接受预订，当动视或 EA 这样做时，人们不会有问题。旁白：电子游戏的 Kickstarter 表现如此出色的一个原因是，当游戏登陆 Steam 时，Steam 会向支持者提供免费密钥。这确实有助于销售，而且 Steam 非常慷慨。人们真的很喜欢 Steam 密钥。如果 Valve 不再允许这样做，视频游戏的启动器将立即变得不那么有价值。另一个想法：Kickstarters 可能会失败或变成欺诈。看，如果您启动游戏但没有成功，嘿，它会发生。但是你需要对你的支持者诚实，如果可能的话，退还他们的一些钱。如果你不这样做，你就会让 Kickstarter 看起来像一个骗局，你就会在背后刺伤你所有的独立开发者。 2. 捐赠（例如 Patreon） 当然，并不是所有的艺术家都会寻找富有的赞助人。其他人则是街头艺人，道路上的勇士，在街角播放他们的音乐，为路人捐款。 Patreon 实现了一个真正的奇迹：它们让您可以在家中的隐私中忙碌，并且您可以通过订阅而不是一次性的方式获得资金。第二部分非常重要。 Kickstarter 往往面向拥有长期粉丝群并建立了一些信任的创作者。 Patreon 往往依赖于有人找到你并对你产生好感，足以让他们分叉信用卡。然后，每个月，您都可以从他们信用评级的强大枫树中汲取几滴宝贵的汁液。受够了那些暂时的善意激增，你就有了一份工作！为了让您的 Patreon 保持盈利，为支持者提供持续不断的内容真的很有帮助。出于这个原因，我相信 Patreon 更适合那些以小块形式制作内容的人，例如作家、艺术家和播客。然而，一些游戏开发商从 Patreon 赚了很多钱，所以必须考虑。我不做 Patreon 是因为我对我的生意老式的“好吧，Boomer”态度：我靠卖东西谋生。你给我钱，我给你一个游戏。但是，当我的业务受到威胁时，该原则将立即消失。附带想法：对于模组和其他用户制作的内容的制造商来说，这是一种很好的（而且早就应该）方式来为他们的努力付出代价。附带想法：我的一个朋友正在考虑以在线艺术家的身份谋生，他正在考虑创建一个 Patreon。然而，要真正赚钱，她需要画很多肮脏版本的卡通人物。我没有真正的教训或目的来说明这一点。我只是觉得很好笑。 3. 企业赞助人（例如 Humble Bundle、Epic Game Store） 当然，您可以通过老式的方式获得赞助：让富有的实体支付一大笔现金。如今，大量现金来自公司。如果你有一款很有吸引力的老游戏，Humble 会以几美分的价格卖出 100 万份。他们得到密友扔进水里，你会得到能见度和很好的检查。如果您有一款看起来很奇特的新独立游戏，Epic Game Store 会为您支付一大笔预付款以获得独家游戏。然后他们获得了出售它的声望，而你得到了赞助人的钱。这款游戏的销量是否真的足以让 Epic 盈利，实际上并没有考虑在内。 （因此，这更像是对古代的赞助，而不是乍一看。）我们在 Humble 上销售了许多游戏，它确实在一些经济不景气的时期承载了我们的业务。我们想在 Epic 上销售游戏，但我们的俗气商品尚未吸引他们。 （嘿 Epic，我们得到了一些非常时髦的老式独立游戏，非常有声望，可以免费获得 Fortnite 数十亿的一小部分！）有技能的开发人员可以使用这种赞助方式。您需要一个非常有前途的标题或一个优质商品库。它不会让你从事业务，但它可以让你在那里。附带想法：Apple 还向那些将游戏放入 Apple Arcade 订阅模式的人发放赞助支票。这对参与者来说很好，但视频游戏的订阅模式将使开发者面临与其他创作者相同的好莱坞会计厄运。如果您获得了订阅服务的大额支票，恭喜您，但不要假装您没有把身后的地球烧焦。这是一个光荣的世界 我毫不讽刺地说：上面的机会是惊人的和令人敬畏的，没有它们我们就没有生意了。每当独立游戏业务变得越来越紧张时，互联网就会想出一种方法来帮助我们在其中生存。我们感到非常非常幸运。这甚至不是赞助的所有可能性。我知道在某些国家/地区，政府会为您提供税收减免和实际支票，以帮助您编写游戏。这项旨在解决全球视频游戏短缺问题的努力应该受到赞扬。当然，如果您没有技能，这些都不会帮助您。如果你不能写出对某些观众有吸引力的东西，没有什么能拯救你。如果您是熟练的独立开发者（或音乐家、艺术家或创作者）并且您的作品很好，那么善良的人们会希望支持您。做一些对一群粉丝说话的东西，他们会向你敞开心扉。然后，幸运的是，他们的钱包。互联网企业家正在想办法帮助这些粉丝成为赞助人。我很少会面带微笑地在这个博客上写东西，但这是其中之一。哦，我有没有提到我有一个 Kickstarter？我的孩子要吃饭了！ ### 我写这些博文是为了引起人们对我们即将推出的游戏 Geneforge 1 - Mutagen 的关注。你也可以在 Twitter 上关注我。
note_en: This is a blog post about making a living writing art. Like, say, indie games. So expect a certain amount of despair.  In ye olden days, many talented people wanted to be artists. However, very few of them sold their art for enough money to make a decent living. So they would get a patron. A patron is a very rich person who is amused by you. They provide you with a tiny chunk of their wealth, and you continue to exist as an artist in order to please them.   (This system came into being many centuries ago, long before modern capitalism existed, so save your Capitalism Iz Bad hot takes for a sunnier day.)  Many very talented people are writing indie games, but the competition is soul-crushing and most gamers don't even bother to play the games they buy. Those of us who have the design chops but don't have a hit have to find patrons.  There are currently a number of routes to patronage. Suppose you want to write your indie 2-D roguelike platformer about an old dying man who wants to learn to smile. Yet, at the same time, you don't want to live in poverty. Here are three big paths to hope.     1. Crowdfunding (e.g. Kickstarter, Fig)  Kickstarter achieved a true miracle: They figured out how to monetize goodwill.  I am a big fan of Kickstarter. I currently am Kickstarting a remaster of our  cult classic Geneforge. It's going well. I am writing this blog post to get more attention for this effort.  Kickstarter is simultaneously a way for people to pre-order your game and a way to find patrons who will pay more substantial amount of money for a single copy. Once, my fans gave me $20-30 per game. Thanks to Kickstarter, richer people who have been my fans for a long time now have a way to pay more and get more in return. They are enabling us to keep writing games.  In ye olden days, if you were a patron for a playwright, you could actually sit on the stage during performances and make fun of the play as it was going on. If you are a serious patron for me, I let you give me ideas to put in the game. You can pay more on our Kickstarter to name a character or design elements of the game.   (By the way, I actually enjoy this a lot. The ideas we've gotten from our backers have been of a VERY high quality and fun to write. But I don't let you do it unless you help me buy food for my kids.)  I expect that we will kickstart every game we write from now on. It really is making that much of a difference for our business. If something about this makes you mad, remember it's basically just taking pre-orders, and people don't have a problem with that when Activision or EA do it.   A Side Thought: One reason video game kickstarters do so well is because Steam gives out free keys to backers of the game when it lands on Steam. This really helps sales and is very generous of Steam. People really like Steam keys. If Valve stops allowing this, kickstarters for video games will instantly become far less valuable.   Another Side Thought: Kickstarters can fail or turn into frauds. Look, if you kickstart a game and it doesn't work out, hey, it happens. But you need to be honest about it to your backers and, if at all possible, refund some of their money. If you don't do this, you make Kickstarter look like a scam, and you are stabbing all your fellow indie devs in the back.     2. Donations (e.g. Patreon)  Of course, not all artists look for wealthy patrons. Others are  buskers, warriors of the road, playing their music on a streetcorner for donations of passers by.  Patreon achieved a true miracle: They let you busk from the privacy of your home, and you get your money as a subscription instead of a one-time thing. The second part is REALLY important.  Kickstarter tends to be for creators who have a long-time fan base and who have built up a bit of trust. Patreon tends to depend on someone finding you and having a surge of goodwill for you, enough that they fork over a credit card. Then, every month, you can tap a few drops of precious sap from the mighty maple of their credit rating. Get enough of those temporary surges of goodwill and you have a job!  To keep your Patreon profitable, it really helps to come out with constant drips of content only for backers. For this reason, I believe that Patreon is better for people who make content in many small chunks, like writers, artists, and podcasters. However,  some game developers are making really good money out of Patreon, so it must be considered.  I don't do Patreon because of my old-fashioned "Ok, Boomer" attitude towards my business: I make a living by selling stuff. You give me money, and I give you a game. Though, the moment my business feels threatened, that principle will go right out the window.   A Side Thought: This is a great (and long overdue) way for  makers of mods and other user-made content to be paid for their efforts.   A Side Thought:  A friend of mine who is trying to make a living as an artist online is considering making a Patreon. However, to really make money, she will need to draw lots of smutty versions of cartoon characters. I have no real lesson or purpose in relating this. I just think it's funny.     3. Corporate Patrons (e.g. Humble Bundle, Epic Game Store)  Of course, you can get patronage in the old-fashioned way: get a rich entity to fork over a wad of cash. These days, the cash wads come from corporations.  If you have an old game that has some appeal, Humble will sell a million copies of it for pennies each. They get chum to throw in the waters, and you get visibility and a nice check.  If you have a new indie game that looks fancy, the Epic Game Store will pay you a huge advance to have an exclusive. Then they get the prestige of selling it, and you get patron bucks. Whether the game actually sells enough to make it profitable for Epic doesn't actually enter into the equation. (Thus, this is more like the patronage of antiquity than it at first appears.)  We have sold many games on Humble, and it really carried our business during some lean times. We want to sell games on Epic, but our tawdry wares have not yet appealed to them. (Hey Epic, we got some really funky old indie games full of prestige, available for a giveaway for but a tiny taste of the Fortnite billions!)  This route to patronage is available to those developers who have skill. You need either a very promising title or a library of quality goods. It won't get you into business, but it can keep you there.   A Side Thought: Apple is also giving out patronage checks to those who put their games into their subscription model  Apple Arcade. This is great for participants, but the subscription model for video games will bring developers to the same  Hollywood Accounting doom faced by other creators. If you get offered a big check to be in a subscription service, congratulations, but don't pretend you aren't scorching the earth behind you.     It's a Glorious World  I say this without irony: The opportunities above are amazing and awesome and without them we wouldn't have a business anymore. Every time the indie games biz gets tighter, the Internet figures out a way to help us survive in it. We feel very, very lucky.  And this isn't even all the possibilities for patronage. I understand in some countries the government will give you tax breaks and actual checks to help write your game. This effort to cure the world's tragic shortage of video games should be applauded.  Of course, none of this will help you if you don't have skill. If you can't write something that has appeal to some audience, nothing will save you.  If you're a skilled indie developer (or musician or artist or creator) and your work is good, kind humans will want to support you. Make something that speaks to a bunch of fans, and they will open their hearts to you. And then, with luck, their wallets. And internet entrepreneurs are figuring out ways to help those fans become patrons.  It's not often I write something in this blog with a smile on my face, but this is one of those times.  Oh, and did I mention  I have a Kickstarter? My kids gotta' eat!  ###   I am writing these blog posts to get attention to our upcoming game,  Geneforge 1 - Mutagen. You can also follow me on  Twitter.
title: 在现代互联网上获得甜蜜的赞助人钱（2020）

note: Jocelyn Bell Burnell 在贵格会家庭长大，从小就相信她和其他人一样有权接受教育。但作为 1940 年代北爱尔兰的一个女孩，她对科学的热情遭到了老师和男学生的敌意。她并没有被吓倒，继续在格拉斯哥大学学习射电天文学，在那里她是许多班级中唯一的女性。 1967 年，伯内尔的一项发现改变了我们对宇宙的看法。作为博士剑桥大学的学生协助天文学家安东尼·休伊什 (Anthony Hewish) 发现了脉冲星——紧凑、旋转的天体，会发出辐射束，就像宇宙灯塔一样。 （一些早期脉冲星数据的可视化作为 Joy Division 的“Unknown Pleasures”的专辑封面而永垂不朽。）但正如上面的简短纪录片所示，世界还没有准备好接受天体物理学的突破可能来自于年轻女子。 Ben Proudfoot 是一名电影制片人，也是 Breakwater Studios 的创始人兼首席执行官。他与奥斯卡提名的 Op-Doc 共同导演了“协奏曲就是一场对话”。 《泰晤士报》致力于向编辑发表各种信函。我们想听听您对此或我们任何文章的看法。这里有一些提示。这是我们的电子邮件：letters@nytimes.com。 Op-Docs 是一个论坛，供独立电影制作人制作的简短、固执己见的纪录片。了解有关 Op-Docs 以及如何提交到该系列的更多信息。在 Facebook、Twitter (@NYTopinion) 和 Instagram 上关注纽约时报的意见部分。
note_en:
Growing up in a Quaker household, Jocelyn Bell Burnell was raised to believe that she had as much right to an education as anyone else. But as a girl in the 1940s in Northern Ireland, her enthusiasm for the sciences was met with hostility from teachers and male students. Undeterred, she went on to study radio astronomy at Glasgow University, where she was the only woman in many of her classes.
 In 1967, Burnell made a discovery that altered our perception of the universe. As a Ph.D. student at Cambridge University assisting the astronomer Anthony Hewish, she discovered pulsars —  compact, spinning celestial objects that give off beams of radiation, like cosmic lighthouses. (A visualization of some early pulsar data is immortalized as  the album art for Joy Division’s “Unknown Pleasures.”)
 But as the short documentary above shows, the world wasn’t yet ready to accept that a breakthrough in astrophysics could have come from a young woman.
  Ben Proudfoot is a filmmaker and the founder and C.E.O. of  Breakwater Studios. He co-directed the Oscar-nominated Op-Doc “ A Concerto Is a Conversation.”
 The Times is committed to publishing   a diversity of letters  to the editor. We’d like to hear what you think about this or any of our articles. Here are some   tips . And here's our email:   letters@nytimes.com .
 Op-Docs is a forum for short, opinionated documentaries by independent filmmakers.  Learn more about Op-Docs and  how to submit to the series. Follow The New York Times Opinion section on  Facebook,  Twitter (@NYTopinion) and  Instagram.

即使在一个习惯了奇怪的东西冲上岸的州——比如可卡因砖、尸体、鲨鱼和未爆炸的军械——周末在佛罗里达搁浅的漂浮装置也让当局采取了双重措施。一个男人从这个装置的顶部舱口里蹦出来，一个临时的人类仓鼠轮子。在从佛罗里达州圣奥古斯丁到纽约的 1,000 多英里的旅程中，他用两条腿的力量只走了 25 英里，如果一切都按计划进行，湾流。这位名叫 Reza Baluchi 的人在周一接受采访时说，他花了数千美元和近十年的时间来改进自制工艺，称为水力舱。它配备了卫星电话、水过滤系统、太阳能电池板、氯丁橡胶潜水服和一堆格兰诺拉麦片和拉面，供他周五从圣奥古斯丁出发，他预计将进行为期三周的旅行。但第二天，当 49 岁的俾路支先生意识到他的备用 GPS 设备和充电电缆丢失时——他说它们被盗了——他缩短了他的荷马之旅。他中止的冒险让海滩游客感到震惊和困惑。 “我打开顶门跳了出去，”他说。 “他们在笑。他们在给我拍照。我知道我在做什么。我又不傻。”弗拉格勒县治安官办公室在 Facebook 上发帖称，周六早上，一些有关人士报告说，一艘船在该县被称为吊床的地区冲刷了海岸。该办公室表示已将此事提交给海岸警卫队，后者将确定该船是否符合海上安全规定。海岸警卫队周一没有立即回应置评请求。水力吊舱仍然搁浅在海滨度假村附近。
出生在伊朗并在美国获得庇护的前职业自行车手俾路支先生说，他希望利用这次旅行的注意力筹集资金，帮助无家可归者和其他慈善事业。他说，多年来，他在水上表演了类似的特技后，收到了包括海岸警卫队在内的困惑反应。 “他们说，'你为什么要这样做？'”住在南佛罗里达州博卡拉顿的俾路支先生说。据 Baluchi 先生说，该水力吊舱由有浮力的铝和塑料球制成，可承载数千磅的重量，他提供了一份他说他已向佛罗里达州提交的船只登记表的副本。在表格上，Baluchi 先生在一个方框中说明了该船具有哪种推进系统，并写道这是舷外类型。他检查了燃料类型的“其他”。在里程表读数和船舶制造商名称的空间中，他写道：“佛罗里达自制船。”佛罗里达州公路安全和机动车辆部周一没有立即发表评论。根据州法律，长度小于 16 英尺的非机动船舶免于获得所有权和注册。俾路支先生说他的水力吊舱长约 6 英尺，宽约 10 英尺。 “现在，我死了，”俾路支先生说，指的是如果他失去了他的水力舱会发生什么。 “我没有车。我把我生活中的一切都投入其中。俾路支先生是一位父亲，自己也曾经无家可归，他对耐力测试并不陌生。此前，他曾因一场慈善越野跑而引起人们的注意。
他说，在早期的水力吊舱模型中，他在太平洋航行了近 400 英里，到达了圣卡塔利娜岛。但是这个装置几年前在佛罗里达州卡纳维拉尔角海岸附近被毁，他说，并补充说海岸警卫队已经建议他应该有一艘船来护送他。但这太昂贵了，Baluchi 先生说，他曾尝试使用椭圆机来为他的水力舱提供动力。在从佛罗里达到纽约的旅程中，Baluchi 先生估计，在当前版本的船只上，他可以达到高达 6 节的速度，其中有一个吊床供他睡觉。他曾计划捕鱼和吃鱼并咀嚼特殊的抗恶心口香糖来控制晕船。他说，为了防止自己在波涛汹涌的海面上被弹跳，他有一个自行车头盔和一个安全带系统。为了保护自己免受烈日的侵袭，俾路支先生用妮基齐林的一张照片遮住了阴影，妮基齐林是一位女演员，曾出演《花花公子》中的“价格合适”的模特。她的贴图照片在他的网站上突出显示，该网站有一个页面供人们跟踪他的进步。 Ziering女士周一在接受采访时说，共同的朋友将她介绍给了俾路支先生。 “他说，'你能像汤姆·汉克斯的电影'Cast Away'中那样做我的威尔逊吗？'”齐林女士说，指的是汉克斯在一个荒岛上的同伴排球。 “我说，'我很荣幸能成为你的威尔逊。'” “如果有鲨鱼，他会拿着长矛，”她说。 “他会骑着仓鼠轮跑，基本上是穿越大洋。”
note_en:
Even in a state accustomed to strange things washing ashore — like cocaine bricks, corpses, sharks and unexploded military ordnance — the floating contraption that beached itself in Florida over the weekend had the authorities doing a double take.
 A man popped out of the top hatch of the contraption, a makeshift human hamster wheel. He had made it only 25 miles on what was supposed to be a 1,000-plus-mile journey from St. Augustine, Fla., to New York, using the power of his two legs and, if all had gone according to plan, the Gulf Stream.
 The man,  Reza Baluchi, said in an interview on Monday that he had spent thousands of dollars and nearly a decade on making improvements to the homemade craft, called a hydro pod. It was equipped with a satellite phone, a water filtration system, a solar array, neoprene wet suits and a stockpile of granola and ramen noodles for when he embarked from St. Augustine on Friday for what he expected would be a three-week trip.
 But the next day, when Mr. Baluchi, 49, realized that his backup GPS device and charging cables were missing — he said they were stolen — he cut short his Homeric odyssey. His aborted adventure startled and bemused beachgoers.
 “I open the top door and jump out,” he said. “They’re laughing. They’re taking pictures of me. I know what I’m doing. I am not dumb.”
 In a Facebook post, the  Flagler County Sheriff’s Office said that some concerned people reported on Saturday morning that a vessel had washed shore in a part of the county known as the Hammock. The office said that it had referred the matter to the Coast Guard, which would determine if the vessel was compliant with marine safety regulations.
 The Coast Guard did not immediately respond to requests for comment on Monday. The hydro pod remained beached near an oceanfront resort.
 Mr. Baluchi, a former professional cyclist who was born in Iran and was granted asylum in the United States, said that he was hoping to use the attention from his trip to raise money to help homeless people and for other charitable causes. Over the years, he said, he has received puzzled reactions — including from the Coast Guard — after performing similar stunts on the water.
 “They say, ‘Why do you do this?’” said Mr. Baluchi, who lives in Boca Raton, in South Florida.
 Constructed from aluminum and plastic balls that are buoyant, the hydro pod can carry several thousand pounds, according to Mr. Baluchi, who provided a copy of a vessel registration form that he said he had filed with the state of Florida.
  On the form, in a box specifying what kind of propulsion system the vessel has, Mr. Baluchi wrote that it was the outboard type. He checked off “other” for the fuel type. And in the space for the odometer reading and the name of the vessel’s manufacturer, he wrote, “Florida homemade boats.”
 The  Florida Department of Highway Safety and Motor Vehicles did not immediately comment on Monday. Under state law, non-motor-powered vessels less than 16 feet in length are exempt from being titled and registered. Mr. Baluchi said his hydro pod was about six feet long and 10 feet wide.
  “Now, I’m dead,” Mr. Baluchi said, referring to what would happen if he were to lose his hydro pod. “I don’t have a car. I put everything in my life in it.”
 Mr. Baluchi, who is a father and was once homeless himself, is no stranger to tests of endurance. He previously drew attention for a  cross-country run for charity.
 In an earlier model of the hydro pod, he said, he traveled nearly 400 miles in the Pacific Ocean, reaching Santa Catalina Island. But the contraption was destroyed several years ago off the coast of Cape Canaveral, Fla., he said, adding that the Coast Guard had advised him that he should have a boat to escort him.
 But that would have been too costly, said Mr. Baluchi, who has experimented with using an elliptical trainer to power his hydro pod.
 For his trip from Florida to New York, Mr. Baluchi estimated that he could have reached speeds of up to 6 knots in the current version of the vessel, which has a hammock for him to sleep in. He had planned to catch and eat fish and to chew special anti-nausea gum to manage seasickness. To protect himself from being bounced around in rough seas, he said, he had a bicycle helmet and a harness system.
 To shield himself from the beating sun, Mr. Baluchi had put up a shade with a photo of  Nikki Ziering, an actress and former model on “The Price Is Right” who has appeared in Playboy. Her pinup photos are featured prominently on his website, which has a page for people to track his progress.
 Ms. Ziering said in an interview on Monday that mutual friends had introduced her to Mr. Baluchi.
 “He said, ‘Can you be my Wilson like in ‘Cast Away,’ the Tom Hanks movie?’” Ms. Ziering said, referring to a volleyball that was Mr. Hanks’s companion on a deserted island. “I said, ‘I would be honored to be your Wilson.’”
  “He’s going to have a spear in case there’s a shark,” she said. “He’s going to run in a hamster wheel, basically, across the ocean.”

本文档旨在作为一个起点，让社区和标准机构参与开发适合标准化的协作解决方案。随着本文档中描述的问题的解决方案在标准轨道上取得进展，我们将保留本文档作为存档，并使用此部分使社区了解最新的标准场所和未来工作的内容位置，以及讨论。当一个本地软件的新版本推出时，用户通常会通过他们的操作系统或他们使用的应用程序商店中的通知来了解。相比之下，网络是常青树，这意味着构建在它之上的软件始终是最新的。因此，没有正式的“时刻”可以让基于 Web 的软件的开发人员通知他们的用户他们的软件从一个版本到另一个版本发生了什么变化（例如，添加了新功能，修复了哪些错误）。提供显示版本历史的标准化方法将使开发人员能够显示这些有用的——有时是关键的——信息。 Manifest.json 更改不会反映在已安装的 PWA 中，直到用户代理认为需要更新。我们打算通过相同的机制更新此功能。当 PWA 开发人员更新 manifest.json 中与版本历史相关的部分时，用户代理应该认为更新是必要的。更新完成后，用户代理可以选择发布有新的更新。用户代理应该有一些机制来指示自用户上次查看更改以来已经推送了新的更改日志版本。大多数 PWA 都会定期更新。通过为最终用户提供有关这些更新的信息的访问权限，他们对应用程序的理解将得到改善。其他好处可能包括： 用户代理可能会选择以类似于公开证书和 cookie 信息的方式公开版本历史记录。在这个动画 GIF 中，用户选择浏览器的三点 (...) 菜单，然后选择“应用信息”，最后选择“版本历史”。这会导致在新窗口中呈现应用程序的版本历史记录。本说明中提出的解决方案是向 Web App Manifest 添加一个可选成员：changelog。
更改日志成员将是一个带有两个键的字典：当前版本 (version) 和定义版本历史 (history) 的一个或多个资源 URL 序列：可选。当前版本名称的人类可读标签。此版本用于表示远程网站所提供内容的版本。如果提供，用户代理将使用它来在此数字更改时发布更新。一个或多个资源的路径。实现者应该根据提供的 MIME 类型信息选择他们可以呈现的第一个资源。注意：历史旨在显示不同格式的变更日志，而不是不同版本的不同变更日志。在大多数情况下，我们怀疑只会提供一种历史资源，但我们也认识到记录版本历史的格式有很多，我们希望尊重这一点。我们建议可接受的格式包括 HTML、RSS/Atom 和 JSONFeed。用户代理可自行决定是否支持其他格式（Markdown 等）。这个可接受的格式列表可能会随着时间的推移而增长。资源旨在作为枚举相同变更日志信息的不同格式的一种方式。当用户选择“版本历史”时应加载的 url。此 URL 必须存在于清单的范围内。如果 manifest-url 是相对 URL，则基本 URL 将是清单的 URL（例如，Twitter 的发行说明）。
提供在 url 中找到的文件的 MIME 类型。至少，可接受的 MIME 类型包括 text/html、application/rss+xml、application/atom+xml 和 application/json。此变更日志信息将定义“1.0.1”的当前版本字符串和单个 HTML 格式的历史资源，可从“https://foo.com/history”获得。版本历史的呈现应该在浏览器窗口中进行。如果版本历史资源的格式不是 HTML，则鼓励实现者将版本历史数据强制转换为语义 HTML 并使用其默认浏览器样式呈现它。如果实现者将数据提要强制转换为 HTML，则应注意对历史进行排序，以使最近的项目位于顶部。实现者可以自由地尝试逐步公开版本信息（例如，手风琴小部件、合理的截断），前提是历史记录中的最新项目被完整显示。 iOS App Store 当前显示版本名称、发布日期以及对每个版本所做更改的简要说明： Microsoft Store 当前显示最新版本随附更改的概述：
预计不会有重大的隐私问题。通过特征检测进行指纹识别的风险很低，因为网页无法判断用户代理是否不支持变更日志，或者用户根本没有触发它。如果用户确实触发了它，我们可能会透露我们是支持此更改日志功能的浏览器。主要的安全问题是用户代理需要处理来自网络的不可信数据源。抛开用户需要从网站安装 PWA 本身——用户代理需要小心他们如何处理数据馈送，以防它们是恶意的。用户代理需要注意不要尝试在特权代码中处理这些不受信任的数据集。正确完成的风险与在相对受信任的站点上运行任意 javascript 的风险相同（因为用户已经安装了 PWA）。 HTML 更改日志不是一个特别的安全问题，因为这不会比标准导航更具风险。
note_en:
This document is intended as a starting point for engaging the community and standards bodies in developing collaborative solutions fit for standardization. As the solutions to problems described in this document progress along the standards-track, we will retain this document as an archive and use this section to keep the community up-to-date with the most current standards venue and content location of future work and discussions.
   When a new version of a piece of native software rolls out users are typically made aware through a notification in their operating system or the app store they use. The web—in contrast—is evergreen, meaning that software built atop it is always up to date. Because of this, there is no formal "moment" in which developers of web-based software can inform their users of what’s changed with their software from release to release (e.g., new features that have been added, which bugs have been fixed). Providing a standardized means of surfacing version history would enable developers to surface this useful—and sometimes crucial—information.
  Manifest.json changes are not reflected in an already installed PWA until the user agent deems an update necessary. We intend for this feature to update via the same mechanism. When a PWA developer updates the section of the manifest.json related to version history, the user agent should deem an update neccesary. After the update is complete, the user agent can choose to publcize that there are new updates.
  User agents should have some mechanism to indicate that a new changelog version has been pushed since the user has last looked at changes.
    Most PWAs are regularly updated. By providing end-users with access to information concerning these updates, their understanding of the app would improve. Other benefits may include:
  A User Agent might choose to expose a version history in a manner similar to how certificate and cookie information is exposed.
 In this animated GIF, the user selects the browser’s triple dot (…) menu, then chooses "App Info," and finally "Version History." This results in the version history for the app being rendered in a new window.  The solution proposed in this explainer is the addition of an optional member to the Web App Manifest:  changelog.
   The  changelog member would be a dictionary with two keys: the current version ( version) and a sequence of one or more resource URLs that define the version history ( history):
   Optional. A human-readable label for current version name. This version is used to represent the version of the content served by the remote website. If provided it will be used by the user agent to publicize updates when this number changes.
  The path to one or more Resources. Implementors should choose the first resource they can render, based on the MIME type information provided.
 Note: history is intended to show different formats of changelog, not different changelogs for different versions.
 In most cases, we suspect that only one  history resource would be provided, but we also recognize that there are many formats for recording version history and we want to be respectful of that. We propose that the acceptable formats include HTML, RSS/Atom, and JSONFeed. Additional formats (Markdown, etc.) may be supported at the User Agent’s discretion. This list of acceptable formats may grow over time.
  Resources are intended as a way to enumerate different formats of the same changelog information.
   The url that should be loaded when users select "Version History." This URL must exist within the  scope of the manifest. If the manifest-url is a relative URL, the base URL will be the URL of the manifest (e.g.,  Twitter’s Release Notes).
  Provides the MIME type of file found at  url. At a minimum, acceptable MIME types include  text/html,  application/rss+xml,  application/atom+xml, and  application/json.
   This  changelog information would define a current version string of "1.0.1" and a single HTML-formatted history resource, available at " https://foo.com/history".
  The rendering of version history should take place within a browser window. If the format of the version history resource is something other than HTML, the implementor is encouraged to  coerce the version history data into semantic HTML and render it using its default browser styles.
 If the implementor is coercing a data feed into HTML, it should take care to sort the history such that the most recent item is at the top.
 Implementors are free to experiment with progressive disclosure of version information (e.g., accordion widgets, sensible truncation), provided the most recent item in the history is displayed in full.
  The  iOS App Store currently displays the version name, a release date, and a brief description of changes made for each version:
  The  Microsoft Store currently displays an overview of changes that came with the latest version:
      No considerable privacy concerns are expected. Fingerprinting via Feature detection is a low risk as a webpage cannot tell if a user agent does not support changelogs, or the user simply did not trigger it. In the case a user does trigger it, we may reveal we are a browser that supports this changelog feature.
  The main security concern is that user agents need to process untrusted data feeds from the web. Putting aside that users need to install the PWA itself from the website - user agents need to be careful how they process the data feeds in case they are malicious. User agents need to take care not to try to process these untrusted data sets in privileged code.
 Done correctly the risk is the same as running arbitrary javascript on an relatively trusted site (since the user already installed the PWA).
 HTML changelogs are not a particular security concern, as this would be no riskier than a standard navigation.
title: 微软提出向 Webmanifest 添加应用变更日志的标准
title_en: Microsoft proposes a standard of adding app changelog to Webmanifest

周二，根据香港全面实施的国家安全法，第一个受审的人被判犯有分裂主义和恐怖主义罪。香港高等法院对24岁的童英杰一案作出判决。他被控驾驶摩托车撞向一群警察，并举着标有“光复香港，时代革命”的抗议标语” 去年 7 月 1 日，即北京在 2019 年反政府抗议活动持续数月后对香港实施全面国家安全立法的第二天。该判决受到密切关注，以寻找未来将如何处理类似案件的迹象。根据安全立法，已有100多人被捕。 Tong 对煽动分裂国家、恐怖主义和危险驾驶的其他指控不认罪。他面临最高终身监禁，预计他的律师将在周四的量刑听证会上争取从轻处罚。审判于 7 月 20 日结束，在没有陪审团的高等法院举行，根据规则，如果需要保护国家机密、涉及外国势力或陪审员的人身安全需要从香港普通法制度中排除这一例外情况。受到保护。审判由香港特首林郑月娥精心挑选的法官主持。童的辩护律师表示，不能仅凭口号就证明童是在煽动分裂国家。
辩方还表示，没有证据表明童是故意的，他避免撞到警察，他的行为不能被视为恐怖主义，因为没有严重的暴力或对社会的危害。虽然香港有自己的立法会，但北京的礼仪立法机构在确定该机构因政治反对而无法通过立法后，将国家安全法强加于这座半自治城市。在此之前，2019 年抗议中国对这座城市事务的影响力越来越大，尽管承诺允许该城市在 1997 年英国统治移交后维持自己的系统 50 年，但抗议活动日益激烈。中国立法机关已要求改变该市立法会的组成，以确保亲北京的压倒性多数，并要求只有那些它认定的“爱国者”才能任职。当局禁止抗议口号“光复香港，时代革命”，称其带有分裂主义内涵。图书馆书籍和学校课程也因涉嫌分裂主义信息而受到调查。香港仅存的民主报纸《苹果日报》上个月被迫停业，作为扩大镇压行动的一部分，四名编辑和记者被以危害国家安全罪名关押，法院拒绝保释。北京驳斥了批评，称它只是在恢复城市秩序，并建立与其他国家相同的国家安全保护措施。
note_en:
The first person to be tried under Hong Kong’s sweeping national security law was found guilty of secessionism and terrorism on Tuesday.
 The Hong Kong High Court handed down the verdict in the case of Tong Ying-kit, age 24. He’s accused of driving his motorcycle into a group of police officers while carrying a flag bearing the protest slogan “Liberate Hong Kong, revolution of our times” on July 1 last year, a day after Beijing imposed sweeping national security legislation on Hong Kong following months of anti-government protests in 2019.
 The verdict was closely watched for indications as to how similar cases will be dealt with in future. More than 100 people have been arrested under the security legislation.
 Tong pleaded not guilty to charges of inciting secession, terrorism and an alternative charge of dangerous driving.
 He faces a maximum sentence of life imprisonment, and his lawyers are expected to argue for a lighter punishment at his sentencing hearing Thursday.
 The trial, which ended July 20, was held in the High Court with no jury, under rules allowing this exception from Hong Kong’s common law system if state secrets need to be protected, foreign forces are involved or if the personal safety of jurors needs to be protected. Trials are presided over by judges handpicked by Hong Kong leader Carrie Lam.
 Tong’s defense lawyer has said it’s impossible to prove that Tong was inciting secession merely by having used the slogan.
 The defense also said there is no evidence that Tong committed the act deliberately, that he avoided crashing into officers and that his actions couldn’t be considered terrorism since there was no serious violence or harm to society.
 While Hong Kong has its own Legislative Council, Beijing’s ceremonial legislature imposed the national security law on the semiautonomous city after it determined the body was unable to pass the legislation itself because of political opposition.
 That followed the increasingly violent 2019 protests against China’s growing influence over the city’s affairs, despite commitments to allow the city to maintain its own system for 50 years after the 1997 handover from British rule.
 China’s legislature has mandated changes to the makeup of the city’s Legislative Council to ensure an overwhelming pro-Beijing majority, and required that only those it determines “patriots” can hold office.
 Authorities have banned the protest slogan “Liberate Hong Kong, revolution of our times,” stating that it has secessionist connotations. Library books and school curricula have also been investigated for alleged secessionist messages.
 Hong Kong’s last remaining pro-democracy newspaper, Apple Daily, was forced out of business last month and a court denied bail for four editors and journalists held on charges of endangering national security as part of the widening crackdown.
 Beijing has dismissed criticisms, saying it is merely restoring order to the city and instituting the same type of national security protections found in other countries.
title: 香港国安法一审有罪判决
title_en: Guilty Verdict in First Trial Under Hong Kong Security Law

N 纽约市一位在大流行爆发后不久被解雇的数据分析师告诉我，她已经申请了失业保险金，然后在接下来的六个月里打电话、发电子邮件和使用社交媒体，试图弄清楚为什么该州劳工部不会把欠她的钱寄给她。费城一位生活在贫困线以下的母亲告诉我，她努力维持政府援助。自己残疾并照顾残疾女儿，她没有得到所有的刺激检查，因为她不定期报税或使用电脑，需要法律援助团体的帮助，以确保她能得到新扩展的孩子-税收抵免付款。一位患有慢性疾病的科罗拉多州系统管理员告诉我，换工作导致他的健康保险意外失效，这导致了一系列关于医疗账单责任的文书工作。他估计他已经花了 100 个小时来解决这个问题。许多美国人都有这样的故事。为了确保安全网能够抓住我们，确保我们的社会保险计划为我们提供保险，确保我们得到支付给山姆大叔的费用，我们作为自己的医疗保健管理员工作。我们自己的税务专家。我们自己的社工。我们自己的残疾法专家。我们自己的儿童抚养倡导者、长期护理代表和公共住房官员。在我十多年的社会政策报道中，我大多将这些故事理解为生活中的事实。存在政府计划。人们必须浏览这些程序。事情就是这样。但在某个时候，我开始将这类行政负担视为“时间税”——对公民征收文书工作、加重负担和脑力劳动，以换取假定存在的帮助他们的利益。这个时间税是公共政策的毒瘤，调解了每个美国人与政府的关系，浪费了人们无数宝贵的时间。问题不在于现代生活伴随着文书工作的麻烦。问题是，美国的福利计划总体上很难，有时甚至不可能让普通公民使用。我们的公共政策是由繁文缛节制定的，使数百万正在努力寻找工作、无法养活孩子、陷入贫困或管理致残健康状况的人纠缠不清。美国政府——无论是由民主党控制的，他们都喜欢过于复杂、经过经济状况调查的政策解决方案；或共和党人，他们喜欢以文书工作作为惩罚；或者两者兼而有之，由于他们集体忽视了政府计划的实施和维护，他们不仅放弃了使福利易于理解和接受。在许多情况下，它故意使系统变得困难，将公共行政的负担转移到个人身上，并阻止数百万美国人寻求援助。政府通过令人困惑的、不公平的官僚摩擦来配给公共服务。当人们没有得到专为他们设计的帮助时，那是他们自己的错。
与富人相比，苦苦挣扎的个人的时间税更糟；黑人家庭比白人家庭大；对病人比对健康人更难。这是一个倒退过滤器，削弱了我们拥有的每一项进步政策。在美国，失业意味着要给州失业保险系统打一百个电话。被车撞意味着成为你自己的医院账单专家。身患残疾意味着进入 Jarndyce v. Jarndyce 类型的法律斗争。需要帮助喂养一个蹒跚学步的孩子意味着填写一份新长度的援助申请。拜登政府正在通过新的儿童税收抵免和其他举措扩大福利国家。国会民主党正在制定新的新政。但是很少有人关注让事情发挥作用，而不是让它们存在。很少有人关注让事情为最需要的人服务——时间、金钱和精神带宽不足的人。需要衡量时间税。它需要被管理。它需要结束。按照设计，这并不容易做到。美国没有统一的社会保障机构。相反，联邦、州和地方办公室管理着数十个具有不同规则和申请流程的不同项目。有些是直接福利计划；其他是复杂的税收支出。有些是权利，如果他们符合条件，每个人都会得到好处；其他是配给福利，提交申请意味着转动轮子并希望最好。一些好处有简单的在线申请；其他的则是老式的纸上噩梦。 （而且许多数字系统与模拟系统一样糟糕。）约翰霍普金斯大学政治学家史蒂文·泰勒斯 (Steven Teles) 令人难忘地将这个系统描述为“杂货政治”。让我们参观地平线。失业保险体系是抵御冠状病毒衰退造成的经济破坏的主要堡垒，使该国的财政得以维持。事实上，它不是堡垒，而是 53 个失业保险系统的拼凑而成，其中许多是为了让用户感到沮丧。它的设计师的目标是“在整个过程中设置尽可能多的毫无意义的障碍，所以人们只会说，‘哦，见鬼去吧；我不会那样做，'”佛罗里达州州长罗恩·德桑蒂斯在大流行期间承认。 “这绝对是为了使支付的索赔数量最少。”估计有 900 万美国人因大流行而失业，但从未获得过一笔失业救济金。或者考虑美国对工薪家庭的援助：补充营养援助计划或食品券；收入所得税抵免；和儿童税收抵免。食品券惠及 2100 万个家庭中的约 4000 万美国人。在许多州，申请它们涉及快速在线请求、快速批准和快速周转才能开始获得福利。但不总是。 SNAP 是工作福利，这意味着被判断为“健全”的成年参与者需要记录他们的工作时间或证明他们正在寻找工作。人们经常因为没有一台正常工作的计算机而被甩掉。 （顺便说一下，这些工作要求并不能促进就业。）至于收入所得税抵免，22% 的符合条件的受助人因为没有报税或报税错报而错过了丰厚的福利。 “信贷资格取决于年底的婚姻状况、收入、收入和公民身份，”税收政策中心指出。 “对于有孩子的人，还有额外的关系和居住测试。资格每年都会有所不同。”
专家担心，数以百万计的儿童——主要是在不稳定、极端贫困的家庭中长大的儿童——将无法享受新的儿童津贴，因为他们的父母不知道该政策的存在，也不会签署。不定期报税的家庭的在线门户网站只有英文，用难以解析的语言编写，并且不适合移动设备使用。不存在纸质或电话注册选项。迄今为止，政府还没有制定计划来帮助最贫困的孩子。时间税也是通过更小、更笨重的程序征收的。获得住房援助是非常痛苦的。考虑加利福尼亚州巴特县的过程。申请人首先需要进入等候名单以获得租金援助。候补名单只定期开放，有时一次只开放几天，届时“在地区报纸上发布公告”并在网站上发布信息。一个人需要中彩票才能上它，然后需要等待——通常是五年。最终选择后，将通过邮件联系有希望的人。接下来是资格预约、申请和最终验证，这可能会或可能不会成功，这个过程“可能需要数周或数月”。许多旨在帮助穷人中最穷的人的计划都有贬低、侵入性和耗时的筛查要求。十多个州要求福利申请人提交药物测试。州妇女、婴儿和儿童计划通常需要面对面的面试和大量的诊所预约，这意味着申请人需要请假并找到前往 WIC 办公室的交通工具，才能获得购买配方奶粉和尿布的帮助。应用程序过长，而且还充满了令人头疼的问题。这是针对贫困老人的补充保障收入接受者必须回答的数十个之一：“您或您的配偶是否出售、转让、处置或赠送任何金钱或其他财产，包括外国的金钱或财产？国家，自申请日月份的第一个时刻起还是在申请日月份之前的 36 个月内？”政府经常要求计划参与者重新申请、重新认证或登录，并提供他们需要和值得的证据。如果人们搞砸了他们的文书工作，等待行政帮助的时间会很长。 “制裁”、“回扣”、扣押和其他惩罚措施的威胁迫在眉睫。我想在这里包括我最喜欢的时间税的例子，文书工作如此荒谬，以至于它具有崇高的品质。加利福尼亚州有一项计划，通过 DMV 为低收入州居民提供低价身份证。要享受该福利，个人需要填写一份 DL 937 表格。但是，个人无法访问和填写此 PDF。它不在网上发布。取而代之的是，申请人必须通过“合格的收入验证者”来这样做，例如无家可归者收容所的社会工作者。 T社会保险计划可能是时间税最明显、最有目的性和最疯狂的地方。但问题更广泛。碎片化、复杂化和个性化——公民由此对国家的行政工作负责——是美国生活的持久特征。
我们国家的卫生系统是一个令人愤怒的泥潭。美国人在人均医疗保险上的支出比任何其他经济体的公民都要多，因为覆盖范围差距很大，健康结果中等，以及繁文缛节。病人被要求争取保险，以确保他们和照顾者照顾他们——协商所涵盖的程序，调查和询问账单做法，并有资格获得政府补贴。该系统对提供者的作用并不比对患者的作用好得多。医生报告说，平均每周填写 37 份保险预授权表格，并且花在文书工作上的时间是花在病人护理上的时间的两倍。我们的税收制度以同样的“你自己想办法”的模式运作，这种模式每年花费公民 90 亿小时。许多其他富裕经济体让其公民知道他们是否欠税或每年向他们发送预先填写的表格进行审查。国税局可以做到这一点，但报税公司的游说和国会的无视维持了悲惨的现状。美国还让在职父母在儿童发展最艰难和最关键的岁月里单独行动：它为 5 至 17 岁的孩子提供公立学校，但对 4 岁或以下的孩子几乎没有或没有。日托点长期供应不足——以及负担得起的日托点供应更加稀缺——促使父母花费数小时和数小时申请不同的中心，并迫使成千上万的父母完全失业。想要接受高等教育？政府将有抱负的大学生分流到复杂的、令人沮丧的融资计划中，不仅让他们背负债务，还背负文书工作。一个有说服力的统计数据：当一所州立旗舰大学向低收入儿童提供“免费”学费保证，而不是承诺提供同等金额的“援助”时，申请率跃升了 42 个百分点，入学率跃升了 15 个百分点。投票同样困难，因为从联邦灾难计划中获得福利、开办企业或从事贸易、重新加入工作中记录重罪、申请进入这个国家或成为公民以及管理残疾。在这个故事中，我通常会介绍美国人花在与我们的政府官僚机构搏斗的时间的淫秽统计。我会证明他们越穷越病重，他们花更多的时间这样做。我要包括的数据表明，由于时间税，许多公民放弃获得福利——或者没有获得他们有资格获得的福利。我会估计美国人作为他们自己的福利管理者所花费的时间的价值，展示这些麻烦如何降低政府计划的功能价值。我要透露的是，由于我们的自由和精干的政府以及我们对官僚主义的怀疑和我们对自力更生的痴迷，美国人比我们在其他国家的同胞花更多的时间做文书工作，尤其是那些拥有社会化医疗系统、国民身份证和良好——资助的官僚机构。
所有这些事情都是真实的，正如社会工作者、社会学家、公民技术专家和公共管理学者所证明的那样——以及数百万普通老年人花费数十个小时拨打政府 800 号码却莫名其妙地只能从上午 9 点到下午 3 点 但是这些假设中的许多都无法用确切的数字来证实。那里没有很多数据。 1980 年的文书工作减少法案确实要求华盛顿衡量和减少监管负担，但它仅适用于某些类型的文书工作。因此，作为一个国家，我们对我们的政府在履行其许多核心职能方面的表现有一个模糊的概念。即使我们没有时间税本身的衡量标准，我们也有充分的证据证明其影响。我们知道数百万美国人没有获得他们有资格获得的抵免额或福利。例如，六分之一的符合条件的人没有收到食品券；在怀俄明州，吸收率仅为 56%。这在很大程度上是由于该程序的管理麻烦。加利福尼亚州的一项新研究发现，家庭在必须重新确认其资格的月份退出 SNAP 的可能性是其六倍。我们还知道，数以百万计的美国人申请医疗补助、失业救济金、福利和其他计划时会遭到“程序性拒绝”，并且由于假定的错误或“欺诈”而被拒绝。 （顺便说一下，当涉及到安全网时，政府没有关于什么构成欺诈的标准。它也没有衡量收入的标准。）总的来说，时间税是累退的。富人计划往往容易、自动且有保障。您无需在个案工作者面前跪拜即可获得 529 大学储蓄计划的好处。您无需在杯子里小便即可为您的房屋、船只或飞机获得税务注销。作为从 401(k) 中获利的先决条件，您不需要找到前伴侣来获得子女抚养费决定。差异如此显着，以至于康奈尔大学政治学家苏珊·梅特勒 (Suzanne Mettler) 表明，许多高收入人群与穷人不同，甚至从未意识到他们正在从政府计划中受益。时间税也是种族主义的，这是对黑人和拉丁裔家庭的偏见的直接体现。种族主义是美国没有像一个世纪前许多欧洲国家那样建立全民福利制度的主要原因。今天，与美国白人不成比例地使用的程序相比，美国黑人不成比例地使用的程序具有更复杂的注册标准和更耗时的申请流程。现金援助申请可能涉及面谈、药物测试和持续遵守工作任务；三分之一的收件人是黑人，另外三分之一是西班牙裔。设置 529 无需申请，也无需每年进行一次试金石测试；该计划的参与者绝大多数是白人。 1965 年的《投票权法案》针对“测试和设备”，例如识字测试，不鼓励少数群体投票。然而，这样的“测试和设备”仍然存在于安全网中。这样，时间税削弱了公众对政府的信心，使人们远离公民生活。人们认为政府不起作用，因为政府不起作用。那么有什么理性的人会相信政府会起作用呢？康奈尔大学政府学教授贾米拉·米切纳 (Jamila Michener) 告诉我，山姆大叔“让人们的生活变得困难”。 “这对民主不利！它不会让人们想要成为政体的一部分。”它还产生了企业、慈善机构、法律援助团体和公民技术组织代表政府执行公共行政的需求；我们所谓的“精益”系统也将官僚主义的工作强加给普通公民。无家可归者收容所花费无数时间为人们招募医疗补助。小学教师帮助家长了解 WIC、SNAP、住房计划和特许彩票。 Intuit 和 H&R Block 等企业从低收入美国人那里榨取时间和退税。 “我总是说我们不应该存在，”公民技术非营利组织 Code for America 的 Tracey Patterson 告诉我。
最后，时间税会造成心理负担。这是政府给人们留下贫困印象的一种方式。它伤害了人们。尽管越来越多的研究表明穷人接受文书工作挑战的能力不如富人，但它仍然如此：贫穷导致认知功能下降，相当于熬夜。斯坦福大学神经学家和压力研究人员罗伯特·萨波尔斯基将美国的贫困和福利计划描述为存在于“恶性循环”中。 “失业、贫困、健康挑战都会产生巨大的压力，是的，这使得更难有效和高效地驾驭需要的东西以获得帮助，”他告诉我。 “如果很明显潜在的帮助来源并不真正关心你会发生什么，那么这种压力就会变得更糟。” H 最富有、最有生产力和最强大的国家最终不仅拥有一个不慷慨的社会政策体系，而且还拥有一个错综复杂、惩罚性强且技术上无能的体系？一个伤害了它声称要帮助的人？故意和设计是答案。这是将“值得”和“不值得”的穷人区分开来的半千年传统的遗产之一。它来自于我们将福利分流给白人家庭和远离黑人家庭的种族主义冲动；我们对大型官僚机构的政治怀疑，源于纳粹主义和共产主义的噩梦，以及我们对新自由主义的信仰；我们坚持国家和地方控制的优越性，这本身就是一种带有种族主义根源的意识形态。对于民主党人来说，它来自对经济状况调查是解决不平等的最佳解决方案的信心，以及对永远、永远给任何人他们可能并不真正需要的好处的恐惧；对于共和党人来说，它的基础是怀疑政府的帮助是否真的帮助了人们，以及利用规则和法规来完成立法可能难以完成的事情的诀窍，例如筛选“平价医疗法案”。 Pamela Herd 和 Donald P. Moynihan 在他们对美国官僚机构和规则的全面调查中认为，随着时间的推移，行政负担已经“被试图使政府功能失调的政治行为者最充分地理解和最有效地利用”。但是设计做出的选择可以通过设计取消，如果华盛顿的民主党想要使税法更公平，扩大安全网，将医疗保险扩展到所有人，并结束儿童贫困，他们应该从让现有的工作更好地开始.就在 COVID-19 关闭之前，我访问了密歇根州

N  a New York City data analyst who had been laid off shortly after the pandemic hit told me she had filed for unemployment-insurance payments and then spent the next six months calling, emailing, and using social media to try to figure out why the state’s Labor Department would not send her the money she was owed.
 A mother in Philadelphia living below the poverty line told me about her struggle to maintain government aid. Disabled herself and caring for a disabled daughter, she had not gotten all of her stimulus checks and, because she does not regularly file taxes or use a computer, needed help from a legal-aid group to make sure she would get the newly expanded child-tax-credit payments.
 A Colorado systems administrator with a chronic medical condition told me that switching jobs had caused an accidental lapse in his health coverage, which led to a cascade of paperwork over responsibility for a medical bill. He estimated that he had spent 100 hours resolving the issue.
 Many Americans have stories like these. To make sure that the safety net catches us, to make sure that our social-insurance programs insure us, to make sure that we get what we pay Uncle Sam for, we work as our own health-care administrators. Our own tax professionals. Our own social workers. Our own disability-law experts. Our own child-support advocates, long-term-care reps, and public-housing officials.
 In my decade-plus of social-policy reporting, I have mostly understood these stories as facts of life. Government programs exist. People have to navigate those programs. That is how it goes. But at some point, I started thinking about these kinds of administrative burdens as the “time tax”—a levy of paperwork, aggravation, and mental effort imposed on citizens in exchange for benefits that putatively exist to help them. This time tax is a public-policy cancer, mediating every American’s relationship with the government and wasting countless precious hours of people’s time.
 The issue is not that modern life comes with paperwork hassles. The issue is that American benefit programs are, as a whole, difficult and sometimes impossible for everyday citizens to use. Our public policy is crafted from red tape, entangling millions of people who are struggling to find a job, failing to feed their kids, sliding into poverty, or managing a disabling health condition.
 The United States government—whether controlled by Democrats, with their love of too-complicated-by-half, means-tested policy solutions; or Republicans, with their love of paperwork-as-punishment; or both, with their collective neglect of the implementation and maintenance of government programs—has not just given up on making benefits easy to understand and easy to receive. It has in many cases purposefully made the system difficult, shifting the burden of public administration onto individuals and discouraging millions of Americans from seeking aid. The government rations public services through perplexing, unfair bureaucratic friction. And when people do not get help designed for them, well, that is their own fault.
 The time tax is worse for individuals who are struggling than for the rich; larger for Black families than for white families; harder on the sick than on the healthy. It is a regressive filter undercutting every progressive policy we have. In America, losing a job means making a hundred phone calls to a state unemployment-insurance system. Getting hit by a car means becoming your own hospital-billing expert. Having a disability means launching into a   Jarndyce v. Jarndyce–type legal battle. Needing help to feed a toddler means filling out a novel-length application for aid.
  The Biden administration is expanding the welfare state, through the new child tax credit and other initiatives. Congressional Democrats are crafting a new New Deal. But little attention is being paid to making things work, rather than making them exist. And very little attention is being paid to making things work for the neediest—people short on time, money, and mental bandwidth.
 The time tax needs to be measured. It needs to be managed. And it needs to end.
  This is not easy to do, by design. The United States has no unified social security agency. Instead, federal, state, and local offices administer dozens of different programs with different rules and application processes. Some are direct-benefit programs; others are complicated tax expenditures. Some are entitlements, where everyone gets the benefit if they qualify; others are rationed benefits, where submitting an application means spinning a wheel and hoping for the best. Some benefits have easy online applications; others are old-fashioned paper nightmares. (And many digital systems are just as bad as the analog ones.) The Johns Hopkins political scientist Steven Teles has memorably described this system as a “ kludgeocracy.”
 Let’s take a  tour d’horizon. The unemployment-insurance system was the primary bulwark against the economic ravages of the coronavirus recession, keeping the country’s finances afloat. It is, in fact, not a bulwark, but a patchwork of 53 unemployment-insurance systems, many of which are meant to frustrate users. Its designers’ goal was to “put as many kind-of pointless roadblocks along the way, so people just say, ‘Oh, the hell with it; I’m not going to do that,’” Florida Governor Ron DeSantis  admitted during the pandemic. “It was definitely done in a way to lead to the least number of claims being paid out.” An estimated 9 million Americans  left jobless by the pandemic never got a single unemployment payment.
 Or consider the tentpoles of American assistance for working families: the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, or food stamps; the earned-income tax credit; and the child tax credit. Food stamps  reach some 40 million Americans in 21 million households. In many states, applying for them involves a quick online request, a quick approval, and a quick turnaround to start getting benefits. But not always. SNAP is  workfare, meaning that adult participants judged to be “able-bodied” need to  log their work hours or demonstrate that they are looking for a job. Folks get thrown off the rolls constantly for, say, not having a functioning computer. (These work requirements do not  boost employment, by the way.)
 As for the earned-income tax credit, 22 percent of eligible recipients  miss out on the generous benefit because they do not file or misfile their taxes. “Credit eligibility depends on marital status at the end of the year, earnings, income, and citizenship status,” the Tax Policy Center  notes. “There are additional tests of relationship and residency for people with children. Eligibility can vary from year to year.”
 Experts worry that millions of children—mostly ones growing up in unstable, extremely poor households—will miss out on the new child allowance because their parents do not know that  the policy exists and will not sign up for it. The  online portal for households that do not regularly file taxes is only in English, written in difficult-to-parse language, and not mobile-friendly. No paper or telephone sign-up option exists. The government has thus far developed no plan to reach the poorest kids.
 The time tax is also imposed through smaller, clunkier programs. Getting housing aid is excruciating. Consider  the process in Butte County, California. An applicant first needs to get on a waitlist for rental assistance. The waitlist opens only periodically, sometimes for just a few days at a time, when a “public announcement is placed in area newspapers” and information is posted on a website. A person needs to win  a lottery to get on it, and then needs to wait—generally for half a decade. When finally selected, hopefuls are contacted by mail. Next comes an eligibility appointment, an application, and finally verification, which might or might not be successful, a process that “could take several weeks or months.”
 Many programs meant to aid the poorest of the poor have demeaning, invasive, and time-consuming screening requirements. More than a dozen states require welfare applicants to submit to a  drug test. State Women, Infants, and Children programs  generally require in-person interviews and numerous in-clinic appointments, meaning applicants need to take time off work and find transportation to a WIC office just to get help buying formula and diapers.
  Applications are overlong and addled with headache-inducing questions too. This is one of dozens that recipients of Supplemental Security Income, aimed at the destitute elderly, must answer: “Have you or your spouse sold, transferred title, disposed of or given away, any money or other property, including money or property in foreign countries, since the first moment of the filing date month or within the 36 months prior to filing date month?”
 The government often asks program participants to reapply, recertify, or log in with evidence of their neediness and worthiness. Should people mess up their paperwork, wait times for administrative help are long. And the threat of “sanctions,” “clawbacks,” garnishments, and other punitive measures looms.
 I want to include my favorite example of the time tax here, a paperwork bother so absurd that it has a quality of the sublime. California has a program to provide low-income state residents with reduced-price ID cards through the DMV. To take advantage of the benefit, a person needs to fill out one Form DL 937. Individuals cannot access and fill out this PDF themselves, however. It is not posted online. Instead, applicants have to do so through a “qualified verifier of income,” such as a social worker at a homeless shelter.
 T  social-insurance programs are perhaps where the time tax is most pronounced, most purposeful, and most deranged. But the problem is broader. Fragmentation, complication, and individualization—whereby citizens become responsible for the administrative work of the state—are persistent features of American life.
 Our country’s health system is an infuriating morass. Americans spend more on health coverage per capita than citizens in  any other economy do, for large coverage gaps, middling health outcomes, and obscene amounts of red tape. The sick are called on to fight for insurance to insure them and carers to care for them—to negotiate what procedures are covered, investigate and interrogate billing practices, and qualify themselves for government subsidies. This system does not work much better for providers than it does for patients. Physicians report  filling out an average of 37 insurance preauthorization forms a week, and spending  twice as much time on clerical work as on patient care.
 Our tax system operates on the same “you figure it out” model, one that costs citizens  9 billion hours a year. Many other wealthy economies let their citizens know if they owe anything in taxes or send them prefilled forms to review once a year. The IRS could do that, but tax-prep companies’ lobbying and Congress’s disregard have maintained the miserable status quo.
 The United States also makes working parents go it alone during the hardest and most crucial years for child development: It provides public school for kids ages 5 through 17, but does little or nothing for kids 4 or younger. A chronic undersupply of day-care spots—and an even more meager supply of affordable day-care spots—spurs parents to spend hours and hours applying to different centers, and forces hundreds of thousands of parents out of the workforce entirely.
 Want to get a higher education? The government shunts aspiring college students into complicated, discouraging financing schemes, saddling them not just with debt but with paperwork. A telling statistic: When  one state flagship university offered a guarantee of “free” tuition to low-income kids, rather than a promise of “aid” worth the same amount, application rates jumped 42 percentage points and enrollment leapt 15 percentage points.
 Voting is similarly difficult, as is getting benefits from a federal disaster program, opening a business or practicing a trade, rejoining the workforce with a felony on your record, applying to enter this country or become a citizen, and managing a disability.
  T  of the story where I would normally introduce an obscene tally of the hours Americans spend wrestling with our government bureaucracy.
 I would demonstrate that they spend more time doing so the poorer and sicker they are. I would include data showing that many citizens give up on getting benefits—or do not receive benefits they qualify for—because of the time tax. I would estimate the  value of the time Americans spend as their own benefits administrators, showing how such hassles reduce the functional value of government programs. I would reveal that Americans, with our freedom and our lean government and our skepticism of bureaucracy and our obsession with bootstrapping, spend more time doing paperwork than our compatriots in other nations, particularly ones with socialized health systems, national identification cards, and well-funded bureaucracies.
  All of these things are true, as social workers, sociologists, civic technologists, and scholars of public administration attest—along with millions of plain-old citizens who have spent dozens of hours calling a government 800-number inexplicably available only from 9 a.m. to 3 p.m. But many of these suppositions cannot be substantiated with hard numbers; there just is not a lot of data out there. The Paperwork Reduction Act of 1980 does obligate Washington to measure and reduce regulatory burdens, but it applies only to certain kinds of paperwork. Thus, as a country, we have a hazy idea of how well our government is performing many of its central functions.
 Even if we do not have a measure of the time tax itself, we have ample evidence of its effects. We know that millions of Americans do not receive the credits or benefits they qualify for. One in six eligible individuals fails to receive food stamps, for example; in Wyoming, the uptake rate is just  56 percent. This is in no small part due to the program’s administrative hassles. A  new study in California found that families are six times more likely to drop out of SNAP the month they have to reconfirm their eligibility.
 We also know that millions of Americans applying for Medicaid, unemployment benefits, welfare, and other programs are subject to “procedural denials,” and have their claims turned down because of supposed errors or “fraud.” (The government has no standard for what constitutes fraud when it comes to the safety net, by the way. It also has no standard measure of income.)
 Taken as a whole, the time tax is regressive. Programs for the wealthy tend to be easy, automatic, and guaranteed. You do not need to prostrate yourself before a caseworker to get the benefits of a 529 college-savings plan. You do not need to urinate in a cup to get a tax write-off for your home, boat, or plane. You do not need to find a  former partner to get a child-support determination as a prerequisite for profiting from a 401(k). The difference is so significant that, as shown by the Cornell political scientist Suzanne Mettler, many high-income people, unlike poor folks, never  even   realize they are benefiting from government programs.
 The time tax is also racist, a straightforward instantiation of bias against Black and Latino families. Racism was a  primary reason that the United States did not create universal benefit systems, as many European countries did a century ago. Today, programs used disproportionately by Black Americans have more complicated enrollment criteria and more time-consuming application processes than programs used disproportionately by white Americans. An application for cash assistance might involve an in-person interview, a drug test, and ongoing compliance with a work mandate;  one third of recipients are Black, and another third Hispanic. Setting up a 529 requires no application and has no annual litmus-testing; the program’s participants are  overwhelmingly white. The Voting Rights Act of 1965  targeted “tests and devices,” such as a literacy tests, that discouraged voting among minority groups. Yet such “tests and devices” live on in the safety net.
 In this way, the time tax undercuts public confidence in government, turning people away from civic life. People think that government cannot work, because government does not work. So what reasonable person would trust government to work? Uncle Sam “is making people’s lives difficult,” Jamila Michener, a professor of government at Cornell, told me. “That is not good for democracy! It doesn’t make people want to be a part of the polity.”
 It also creates the need for businesses, charities, legal-aid groups, and civic-technology organizations to perform public administration on behalf of the government; our allegedly “lean” system forces the work of bureaucracy onto private citizens too. Homeless shelters spend countless hours enrolling people in Medicaid. Elementary-school teachers help parents navigate WIC, SNAP, housing programs, and charter lotteries. Businesses such as Intuit and H&R Block leech time and tax refunds from low-income Americans. “I always say that we should not exist,” Tracey Patterson of Code for America, a civic-technology nonprofit, told me.
  Finally, the time tax takes a  psychological toll. It is a way the government impresses poverty on people. It  hurts people. It does so despite a growing pool of research showing that poor folks are less capable of taking on paperwork challenges than their richer counterparts: Poverty causes a dip in cognitive function equivalent to  pulling an all-nighter.
 Robert Sapolsky, a Stanford neurologist and researcher of stress, describes American poverty and American benefit programs as existing in a “vicious cycle.” “Unemployment, poverty, health challenges all generate enormous stress and, yes, this makes it harder to effectively and efficiently navigate what is needed in order to get help,” he told me. “That stress is then made far worse if it is abundantly clear that that potential source of help doesn’t really care what happens to you.”
 H  wealthiest, most productive, and most powerful country end up with not just an ungenerous system of social policy but a convoluted, punitive, and technologically inept one? One that hurts the people it purports to help?
 On purpose and by design is the answer. It is one legacy of the  half-millennium-old custom of separating the “deserving” and “undeserving” poor. It comes from our racist impulse to shunt benefits to white families and away from Black families; our political skepticism of big bureaucracies, informed by the nightmares of Nazism and communism, as well as our faith in neoliberalism; and our insistence on the superiority of state and local control, itself an ideology with racist roots. For Democrats, it comes from a confidence in means-testing as the best solution to inequality and a fear of ever, ever giving anyone a benefit they might not  really need; for Republicans, its foundation is a skepticism that government help really helps people and a knack for using rules and regulations to accomplish what might be hard to accomplish through legislation, such as the winnowing of the Affordable Care Act.
 Over time, administrative burdens have been “best understood—and most effectively used—by political actors seeking to make government dysfunctional,” argue Pamela Herd and Donald P. Moynihan in their  comprehensive survey of American bureaucracy and rules.
 But choices made by design can be undone by design, and if Democrats in Washington want to make the tax code fairer, expand the safety net, extend health insurance to everyone, and end child poverty, they should start with making what already exists work better.

note:
跳转到导航 跳转到搜索 企业收集和出售在线时间数据的经济系统 监视资本主义是一种以个人数据商品化为中心，以盈利为核心目的的经济系统。正如 Shoshana Zuboff 所描述的那样，监视资本主义的概念是随着谷歌 AdWords 领导的广告公司看到使用个人数据更精确地定位消费者的可能性而产生的。 [1] 工业资本主义剥削和控制自然并带来毁灭性后果，而监视资本主义则以极权秩序为发展终点，剥削和控制人性。 [2] 增加的数据收集可能对个人和社会具有各种优势，例如自我优化（量化自我），[3] 社会优化（例如通过智慧城市）和优化服务（包括各种 Web 应用程序）。然而，在资本主义核心盈利动机的背景下收集和处理数据可能对人类自由、自主和福祉构成威胁。资本主义已经开始专注于扩大对数据收集和数据处理开放的社会生活的比例。 [3] 这可能会对社会的脆弱性和控制以及隐私产生重大影响。资本主义的经济压力正在推动在线连接和监控的加强，社会生活空间变得对企业行为者开放至饱和，旨在赚取利润​​和/或规范行动。因此，在知道有针对性的广告的可能性之后，个人数据点的价值增加了​​。 [4] 因此，不断上涨的数据价格限制了社会上最富有的人购买个人数据的可及性。 [5] Shoshana Zuboff 写道，“分析海量数据集最初是通过发现人类和系统行为中未来模式的概率来减少不确定性的一种方式。” [6] 2014 年，文森特·莫斯科 (Vincent Mosco) 将向广告商推销有关客户和订阅者的信息称为监视资本主义，并在旁边记录了监视状态。 [7] Christian Fuchs 发现监视国家与监视资本主义融合在一起。 [8] 同样，Zuboff 表示，与国家安全机构的高度隐形合作安排使问题进一步复杂化。根据 Trebor Scholz 的说法，公司招募人员作为这种资本主义的线人。 [9] 祖博夫将工业资本主义的大规模生产与监视资本主义进行了对比，前者与其作为其消费者和雇员的人口相互依存，后者则捕食既不是其消费者也不是其雇员且在很大程度上不了解其程序的依赖人群。 [10] 他们的研究表明，资本主义对分析海量数据集的补充使其初始目的发生了意想不到的转变。 [1] 监视一直在改变信息经济中的权力结构，有可能将权力平衡从民族国家转移到采用监视资本主义逻辑的大公司。 [11] Zuboff 指出，监视资本主义超越了私营公司的传统制度领域，不仅积累了监视资产和资本，而且在没有有意义的同意机制的情况下积累了权利和运作。 [10] 换句话说，在某些时候，分析海量数据集不仅由国家机构执行，也由公司执行。在 Zuboff 的研究中，她声称这两家公司；谷歌和 Facebook 发明了监视资本主义并将其转变为“一种新的积累逻辑”。 [1] [12] [13] 这种变化导致两家公司都收集了大量关于其用户的数据点，其核心目的是盈利。通过将这些数据点出售给外部用户（尤其是广告商），它已成为一种经济机制。对海量数据集的分析与将这些数据集用作市场机制相结合，形成了监视资本主义的概念。监视资本主义被认为是新自由主义的继承者。 [14] [15] 电影斯诺登的创作者奥利弗·斯通指出，基于位置的游戏 Pokémon Go 是“新兴现象和监视资本主义示范的最新迹象”。斯通批评该游戏，理由是其用户的位置不仅用于游戏目的，而且还用于检索有关其玩家的更多信息。通过跟踪用户的位置，游戏收集的信息远不止用户的姓名和位置：“它可以访问您的 USB 存储设备的内容、您的帐户、照片、网络连接和手机活动，甚至可以激活您的手机，当它处于待机模式时。”然后这些数据可以被谷歌等公司分析和商品化（他们对游戏的开发进行了大量投资），以提高定向广告的有效性。 [16] [17]
监视资本主义的另一个方面与其对政治竞选的影响有关。数据挖掘者检索到的个人数据可以使各家公司（最著名的是剑桥分析公司）改进政治广告的针对性，这超出了先前 Surveillance Capitalist 运营的商业目的。这样，政党就有可能制作更有针对性的政治广告，以最大限度地扩大对选民的影响。然而，多克托罗写道，滥用这些数据集“将导致我们走向极权主义”。 [18] 这可能类似于公司治理，图罗写道，“公司权力的中心地位是数字时代核心的直接现实。” [3] [19] : 17 在祖博夫的理论中，监视资本主义是一种新颖的市场形式和资本主义积累的特定逻辑。在她 2014 年的文章《数字宣言：大数据作为监视资本主义》中，她将其描述为基于“现实”商品化及其转化为用于分析和销售的行为数据的“信息资本主义的彻底脱离和提取变体”。 [20] [21] [22] [23] 为了进一步理解这个术语，澳大利亚埃迪斯科文大学的 Donell Holloway 说“监视资本主义描述了一个市场驱动的过程，其中出售的商品是您的个人数据，捕获和生产这些数据依赖于对互联网的大规模监视。” [24] 在随后 2015 年的一篇文章中，祖博夫分析了这种资本主义突变的社会影响。她区分了监视资产、监视资本和监视资本主义，以及它们对全球计算机中介架构的依赖，她称之为“大他者”，这是一种分布式且基本上没有争议的新权力表达方式，构成了提取、商品化和威胁自由、民主和隐私等核心价值观的控制。 [25] [3] 根据 Donell Holloway（2019 年）的说法，“目前，最大的‘大他者’参与者是谷歌、亚马逊、Facebook 和苹果……他们一起收集和控制有关我们行为的数量无与伦比的数据，这[24] Shoshana Zuboff 认为，监控资本主义是谷歌和后来的 Facebook 的先驱，与一个世纪前福特和通用汽车开创的大规模生产和管理资本主义非常相似，现在已经[10] 在牛津大学 2016 年发表的演讲中，Zuboff 确定了监视资本主义的机制和实践，包括制造“预测产品”以在新的“行为期货市场”中销售。她介绍了这个概念“监视剥夺”，并认为它挑战了自决的心理和政治基础，因为它将权利集中在监视制度中。这被描述为是“从上而下的政变”。 [26] 在 2019 年 3 月接受《哈佛公报》采访时，Zuboff 表示她对这个问题的关注可以追溯到 2007 年。Zuboff 将“监视资本主义”定义为“单方面声称私人人类经验是免费的翻译原材料转化为行为数据……然后将这些数据计算并打包为预测产品，并出售到行为期货市场——商业客户对了解我们现在、不久和以后将要做什么具有商业利益。” [27] 她接着说，最初的问题的一部分是“我们冲到互联网上期待赋权、知识民主化和帮助解决实际问题，但监视资本主义实在是太有利可图了，无法抗拒。” [27] Shoshana Zuboff 的著作 The Age of Surveillance Capitalism [28] 于 2019 年 1 月 15 日出版。它详细研究了监视资本主义的空前力量以及强大公司对预测和控制我们行为的追求。 [29] Zuboff 确定了监视资本主义逻辑中的四个关键特征，并明确遵循 Google 首席经济学家 Hal Varian 确定的四个关键特征：[30]
使用技术基础设施对其用户和消费者进行持续实验。祖博夫将要求监视资本家的隐私或游说结束互联网上的商业监视与要求亨利·福特手工制作每个 T 型车进行比较，并指出此类要求是违反实体生存基本机制的生存威胁。 [10] 祖博夫警告说，自决原则可能会因“无知、习得性无助、注意力不集中、不便、习惯或漂泊”而丧失，并指出“我们倾向于依赖从过去提炼出来的心智模型、词汇和工具灾难”，指的是 20 世纪的极权主义噩梦或镀金时代资本主义的垄断掠夺，为应对这些早期威胁而制定的对策不足以甚至不适合应对新挑战。 [10] 她还提出了一个问题：“我们是信息的主人，还是信息的奴隶？”并指出“如果数字未来要成为我们的家，那么我们必须做到这一点。” [31] 在她的书中，Zuboff 讨论了工业资本主义和监视资本主义之间的区别。祖博夫写道工业资本主义剥削了自然，监视资本主义剥削了人性。 [32] 政治经济学家约翰·贝拉米·福斯特（John Bellamy Foster）和罗伯特·W·麦克切斯尼（Robert W. McChesney）也使用了“监视资本主义”一词，尽管含义不同。他们在 2014 年发表在 Monthly Review 的一篇文章中，用它来描述金融化“对数据的永无止境的需求”的表现，他们解释说是美国引入的“金融资产相对于 GDP 的长期增长投机”。 1980 年代从军工复合体和广告业演变而来的按行业和政府划分的州。 [33] 许多组织一直致力于在新的监视资本主义 [34] 中争取言论自由和隐私权，并且各个国家政府都颁布了隐私法。在 2018 年初发生的 Facebook-Cambridge Analytica 数据丑闻之后，政府对监视资本主义危险的关注暴露出来。 [5] 为了应对大规模监视的滥用，多个州采取了预防措施。例如，欧盟已经对这些事件做出反应，并限制了其关于滥用大数据的规则和规定。 [35] 在这些规则下，监视资本主义变得更加困难，这些规则被称为通用数据保护条例。 [35] 然而，对许多国家来说，实施防止滥用大规模监视的预防措施很困难，因为它需要对系统进行结构性改变。 [36] [37]
布鲁斯·斯特林 (Bruce Sterling) 2014 年在 Strelka 研究所的演讲“物联网的史诗般的斗争”[38] 解释了消费品如何成为跟踪人们日常生活的监视对象。在他的演讲中，Sterling 强调了跨国公司之间的联盟，这些公司开发了基于物联网的监控系统，为监控资本主义提供了支持。 [38] [39] [40] 2015年，Tega Brain和Surya Mattu的讽刺作品Unfit Bits鼓励用户颠覆Fitbits收集的健身数据。他们提出了通过将设备连接到节拍器或自行车车轮上来伪造数据集的方法。 [41] [42] 2018 年，Brain 与 Sam Lavigne 创建了一个名为 New Organs 的项目，该项目收集人们在线和离线被监控的故事。 [43] [44] 2019 年的纪录片《The Great Hack》讲述了一家名为 Cambridge Analytica 的公司如何利用 Facebook 操纵 2016 年美国总统大选的故事。对由黑盒算法排序的用户和新闻提要的广泛分析被认为是问题的主要来源，Zuboff 的书中也提到了这一点。 [45] 同样在 2020 年，COVID 大流行通过电子和生物识别接触者追踪为监视资本主义提供了新途径，特别是在全球南方。疾病控制预防中心 (CDC) [46] 制定了数字接触者追踪流程和移动应用程序的指南。 Apple 和 Google 合作制定了隐私保护联系追踪 [47] 声明，为个人开发者、公共卫生部门和其他可能利用其技术创建应用程序和流程以追踪 COVID-19 的传播和存在的人提供指导在社区和地区。 ^ abc Zuboff, Shoshana（2019 年 1 月）。 “监视资本主义和集体行动的挑战”。新劳工论坛。 28 (1): 10–29。 doi：10.1177/1095796018819461。 ISSN 1095-7960。 S2CID 159380755。^ abcd Canry, Nick（2016 年 9 月 23 日）。 “连接的代价：‘监视资本主义’”。谈话。原始存档于 2020 年 5 月 20 日。 ^ John Wiley & Sons, Inc.（2018 年 6 月 1 日），数据分析和大数据：第 5 章：数据分析过程：幕后的伟大工作，第 77–99 页，doi：10.1002/9781119528043.ch5，ISBN 978-1-119-52804-3
